name: Boltz
tagline: Open-source biomolecular structure and affinity prediction
metaDescription: Boltz is an open-source family of models (Boltz-1, Boltz-2) that predict biomolecular structures and binding affinities from protein and ligand inputs.
overview: Boltz is a family of open-source models for predicting biomolecular structures and interactions. It is released under the MIT license, which allows both academic and commercial use, and ships its code and model weights openly.

The project includes two models. Boltz-1 was described as the first fully open-source model to approach AlphaFold3 accuracy. Boltz-2 goes further by jointly modeling complex structures and binding affinities, and reports approaching the accuracy of physics-based free-energy perturbation (FEP) methods while running much faster.

You run Boltz from the command line by pointing it at an input file that describes the proteins and ligands you want to model. It can generate the multiple sequence alignments it needs automatically through an MSA server, and it reports both structure predictions and binding results.
features: Predicts biomolecular complex structures from protein and ligand inputs
Boltz-2 jointly models complex structures and binding affinities
Reports binding affinity as log10(IC50) in micromolar units, plus a binding probability score
Automatic MSA generation through an MSA server integration
Fully open source under the MIT license, for academic and commercial use
GPU acceleration via NVIDIA cuEquivariance kernels, with a CPU-only option

gettingStarted.intro: Install Boltz from PyPI, then run a prediction against an input file that describes your biomolecules.

steps.heading: Install Boltz
steps.body: Install from PyPI with the CUDA extra for GPU support. Remove the [cuda] extra for CPU-only or non-CUDA hardware, which is significantly slower.

steps.heading: Run a prediction
steps.body: Point the predict command at your input path. The --use_msa_server flag lets Boltz generate the multiple sequence alignments it needs automatically.

steps.heading: See all options
steps.body: List every available prediction flag and setting from the command line.

useCases: Predict the structure of a protein-ligand complex from sequence and molecule inputs
Estimate binding affinity to help rank candidate ligands during optimization
Screen candidates using the binding probability score during hit discovery
Run structure and affinity prediction locally as an open-source alternative to hosted tools
